key_insight: Atomically defect-free FeS2(100) terraces can organize L-cysteine into two distinct supramolecular phases, showing that pristine mineral surfaces—not just defects—can drive prebiotic self-assembly.
engineering_relevance: The study suggests that surface order and electronic structure of mineral catalysts can be engineered to control molecular organization and reactivity in prebiotic or catalytic interface systems.
method: STM/STS to image and probe L-cysteine assemblies on FeS2(100) terraces
DFT calculations to interpret intermolecular interactions and substrate effects
Comparison of compact and labile supramolecular phases on defect-free surfaces
evidence_signal: The abstract reports atomically ordered terraces and two experimentally observed L-cysteine phases, supported by STM/STS measurements and DFT-based trimer interaction analysis.
watch_item: The assemblies are described on defect-free terraces under controlled conditions, so stability and relevance on more realistic, defect-rich or chemically complex primordial surfaces may differ.
paper_title: Prebiotic Chemistry Assemblies of L-Cysteine on Defect-Free Pyrite Terraces
